title: Accessing Vector Elements
description: Indexing vectors with positive/negative integers, logical vectors, and names.

slides.title: Indexing with Positive Integers 🔢
slides.content:
You can extract elements by their position using square brackets `[ ]`. In R, indexing starts at 1 (not 0).

```r
x <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)
x[1]    # 10
x[3]    # 30
x[c(1, 3, 5)]  # 10 30 50
```

You can also repeat indices:
```r
x[c(1,1,2)]  # 10 10 20
```

quizData.question: What is the result of `x <- c(5,10,15); x[2]`?
quizData.explanation: Indexing starts at 1, so x[2] is the second element: 10.

slides.title: Negative Integers: Excluding Elements ➖
slides.content:
Using negative indices excludes those positions:

```r
x <- c(10, 20, 30, 40)
x[-2]       # 10 30 40 (exclude second)
x[c(-1,-3)] # 20 40 (exclude first and third)
```

You cannot mix positive and negative indices in the same subset.

slides.title: Logical Indexing 🎯
slides.content:
You can use a logical vector of the same length to select elements where the condition is TRUE.

```r
x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
sel <- c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)
x[sel]  # 1 3 5
```

More commonly, you create the logical vector on the fly:

```r
x[x > 3]  # 4 5
```

slides.title: Logical Subsetting
slides.codeSnippet:
ages <- c(25, 30, 35, 40, 45)
adults <- ages[ages >= 30]
print(adults)

even <- ages[ages %% 2 == 0]
print(even)

quizData.question: Given `v <- c(3,6,9,12)`, what does `v[v %% 3 == 0]` return?
quizData.explanation: All numbers are divisible by 3, so the logical vector is all TRUE, returning the entire vector.

slides.title: Indexing by Name 🏷️
slides.content:
If a vector has names, you can use those names inside brackets:

```r
x <- c(a=10, b=20, c=30)
x['b']     # 20
x[c('a','c')]  # 10 30
```

slides.title: Name Indexing
slides.codeSnippet:
product <- c(apple=0.5, banana=0.3, cherry=0.2)
print(product['banana'])
print(product[c('cherry','apple')])

slides.title: Subsetting Practice
slides.content: Create a vector of temperatures: 72, 68, 75, 80, 65. Extract temperatures above 70. Then extract all except the third temperature.
slides.codeSnippet:
temps <- c(72, 68, 75, 80, 65)
hot <- temps[temps > 70]
print(hot)
except_third <- temps[-3]
print(except_third)
